units.sub: The Americas, Europe & Africa Before Contact

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 1
topics.cedContent: Before European contact, the Americas were home to diverse and complex societies. European exploration was driven by economic competition, religious zeal, and technological advances. The meeting of Native American, European, and African peoples in the Americas after 1492 would transform all three worlds.

topics.title: Native American Societies Before European Contact
topics.cedContent: The spread of maize cultivation from present-day Mexico northward supported economic development, settlement, advanced irrigation, and social diversification. Societies in the Great Basin and Plains developed mobile lifestyles in response to aridity. In the Northeast, Mississippi Valley, and Atlantic seaboard, societies developed mixed agricultural and hunter-gatherer economies favoring permanent villages. Northwest and California societies supported themselves by hunting and gathering, with some developing settled communities from ocean resources.

topics.title: European Exploration in the Americas
topics.cedContent: Improvements in maritime technology and more organized methods for conducting international trade, such as joint-stock companies, supported European exploration. Motivation for exploration and conquest included desire to reach Asian markets, economic competition between nation-states, and spreading Christianity. Spain sent Columbus and later conquistadors to challenge Portuguese expansion and bring wealth back to Spain.
illustrativeExamples: Spanish conquest of Aztec and Inca

topics.title: Columbian Exchange, Spanish Exploration & Conquest
topics.cedContent: Spanish exploration and conquest were accompanied by widespread deadly epidemics that devastated native populations. New crops (maize, potatoes) contributed to population growth in Europe. Introduction of livestock wreaked havoc on Indigenous lands while introduction of the horse revolutionized hunting and warfare. Native American populations declined by up to 90% in many regions.
illustrativeExamples: Introduction of horses to Plains peoples

topics.title: Labor, Slavery & Caste in the Spanish Colonial System
topics.cedContent: The Spanish developed a caste system (castas) that incorporated and carefully defined the status of the diverse population of New Spain — peninsulares, criollos, mestizos, mulattoes, Native Americans, and enslaved Africans. The encomienda system marshaled Native labor for agriculture and silver mining. The need for labor led to development of the Trans-Atlantic slave trade.

topics.title: Cultural Interactions Between Europeans, Native Americans & Africans
topics.cedContent: As European encroachments on Native lands increased, native peoples sought to defend their lands and ways of life through military resistance and diplomatic negotiations. Europeans and Native Americans had divergent worldviews on land ownership (communal vs. private), religion (polytheistic/animistic vs. monotheistic), and gender roles. Europeans like Sepúlveda and Las Casas debated the treatment of Indigenous people. Some groups allied with Europeans; others resisted through the Pueblo and Taino revolts.
illustrativeExamples: Allied tribes helped Cortés defeat Aztec
Adoption of Indigenous words: canoe, hammock, moccasin

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 2
topics.cedContent: European colonization of North America led to the development of distinct colonial societies shaped by geography, religion, and labor systems. Interactions between Europeans, Native Americans, and Africans created new and complex social hierarchies.
illustrativeExamples: English, French, Dutch, Spanish colonial patterns

topics.title: European Colonization
topics.cedContent: French and Dutch colonial efforts involved relatively few Europeans and relied on trade alliances and intermarriage with Native peoples. English colonization attracted a comparatively large number of male and female British migrants, as well as others from across Europe. The headright system attracted settlers; indentured servitude provided initial labor but transitioned to hereditary racial slavery after Bacon's Rebellion (1676).

topics.title: The Regions of British Colonies
topics.cedContent: The middle colonies supported a flourishing export economy based on cereal crops and attracted a broad range of European settlers. The colonies of the southern Atlantic coast used long growing seasons to develop plantation economies. New England colonies developed around Puritan town meeting governance, subsistence farming, and strong communal identity. The colonies developed distinct regional cultures shaped by religion, economy, and geography.
illustrativeExamples: New England: Puritan theocracy, town meetings
Middle colonies: diverse, commercial farming (Penn's 'Holy Experiment')
Chesapeake: tobacco plantations, House of Burgesses
Carolina low country: rice and indigo with enslaved labor

topics.title: Transatlantic Trade
topics.cedContent: The British government increasingly attempted to incorporate its North American colonies into a coherent, hierarchical imperial structure through mercantilist policies including the Navigation Acts. Continuing trade with Europeans increased the flow of goods in and out of American Indian communities, stimulating cultural and demographic changes. The doctrine of salutary neglect in practice allowed colonial assemblies significant autonomy.
illustrativeExamples: Growing merchant class in port cities

topics.title: Interactions Between American Indians and Europeans
topics.cedContent: British conflicts with American Indians over land, resources, and political boundaries led to military confrontations. American Indian resistance to Spanish colonizing efforts, particularly after the Pueblo Revolt, led to Spanish accommodation of some aspects of Native culture. Fur trade alliances reshaped Native societies; King Philip's War (1675–76) devastated New England Native populations.

topics.title: Slavery in the British Colonies
topics.cedContent: As chattel slavery became the dominant labor system in many southern colonies, new laws created a strict racial system that distinguished between free and enslaved people. Slave codes codified racial hierarchy and stripped enslaved people of legal rights. The shift from indentured servitude to hereditary racial slavery accelerated after Bacon's Rebellion. Enslaved people resisted through a variety of means including revolts.

topics.title: Colonial Society and Culture
topics.cedContent: The British colonies experienced gradual Anglicization over time, developing autonomous political communities based on English models. Colonists' resistance to imperial control drew on local experiences of self-government and evolving ideas of liberty. The First Great Awakening (1730s–40s) challenged established religious hierarchies and fostered a sense of common identity. Transatlantic print culture expanded literacy and political ideas.
illustrativeExamples: Jonathan Edwards 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God'

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 3
topics.cedContent: Tensions between Britain and the American colonies over taxation and representation led to revolution and the creation of a new nation. Enlightenment ideals of natural rights and popular sovereignty shaped the founding documents and ongoing debates about the nature of American democracy.

topics.title: The Seven Years' War (French and Indian War)
topics.cedContent: Britain achieved a major expansion of its territorial holdings by defeating the French, but at tremendous expense, setting the stage for conflicts with colonists over how to pay for the war. After the British victory, imperial officials' attempts to prevent colonists from moving westward generated colonial opposition to the Proclamation of 1763. The war gave colonists military experience and a growing sense of identity distinct from Britain.
illustrativeExamples: Treaty of Paris (1763) — France cedes Canada
Proclamation of 1763 — colonists prohibited from moving west
Albany Plan of Union (1754) — Franklin's proposal

topics.title: Taxation Without Representation
topics.cedContent: Colonial leaders based their calls for resistance on arguments about the rights of British subjects, the rights of the colonists as human beings, and ideas of popular sovereignty. The effort for independence was energized by colonial leaders such as Benjamin Franklin and Samuel Adams, as well as by popular movements including boycotts. Men and women mobilized in large numbers to resist parliamentary taxation.

topics.title: Philosophical Foundations of the American Revolution
topics.cedContent: The colonists' belief in the superiority of republican forms of government based on the natural rights of the people found expression in Thomas Paine's Common Sense and the Declaration of Independence. Enlightenment ideas — Locke's natural rights, social contract theory — shaped revolutionary ideology. Religion also strengthened Americans' view of liberty through concepts like covenant theology.
illustrativeExamples: John Locke's Two Treatises of Government
'Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness'

topics.title: The American Revolution
topics.cedContent: The Revolution was characterized by Loyalist opposition, debates about the nature of republican government, and various groups seeking to use the ideology of the Revolution to support their own goals. French alliance after Saratoga (1777) was crucial. The Treaty of Paris (1783) recognized US independence. Democratic ideals spread globally but limits of revolutionary change remained for enslaved people, women, and Native Americans.

topics.title: The Influence of Revolutionary Ideals
topics.cedContent: In response to women's participation in the Revolution, Enlightenment ideas, and women's appeals, new ideas about women's roles emerged, including 'republican motherhood.' Revolutionary ideals led to gradual emancipation in the North and calls for abolition. However, the limits of revolutionary change remained — enslaved people, women, and Native peoples were largely excluded from expanded rights.
illustrativeExamples: Vermont banned slavery in its constitution
Growth of abolitionist societies in North

topics.title: The Articles of Confederation
topics.cedContent: The Articles of Confederation unified the newly independent states, creating a central government with limited power. Articles gave Congress no power to tax, regulate commerce, or enforce laws. Successes included the Land Ordinance of 1785 and Northwest Ordinance (1787), which banned slavery north of the Ohio River. Shays' Rebellion (1786) exposed weaknesses and led directly to the Constitutional Convention.
illustrativeExamples: Land Ordinance of 1785 — systematic land sale
Northwest Ordinance (1787) — banned slavery in territories
Each state had one vote regardless of size
Congress could not tax — relied on state contributions

topics.title: The Constitutional Convention & Debates over Ratification
topics.cedContent: The Constitutional Convention compromised over the representation of slave states in Congress (Three-Fifths Compromise) and the role of the federal government. In the ratification debate, Anti-Federalists opposing ratification battled with Federalists, whose position was articulated in the Federalist Papers. The Bill of Rights was added to secure ratification by Anti-Federalists who feared concentrated power.

topics.title: The Constitution
topics.cedContent: The Constitution established a federal structure balancing state and national power. Enumerated powers and the elastic clause defined the scope of federal authority. Judicial review was later established by Marbury v. Madison (1803). The amendment process provided flexibility. The Bill of Rights protected individual liberties from federal overreach.

topics.title: Shaping a New Republic
topics.cedContent: War between France and Britain resulting from the French Revolution presented challenges to the United States over issues of neutrality, impressment, and trade. Washington set key precedents including the Cabinet system and serving only two terms. Hamilton's financial plan (national bank, assumption of state debts) clashed with Jefferson's agrarian vision, creating the first political parties. The Whiskey Rebellion demonstrated federal authority.

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 4
topics.cedContent: The early republic faced challenges of national unity, territorial expansion, and growing sectional tensions over slavery. The Market Revolution transformed the economy and society while democratic reforms expanded political participation for white men but excluded others.

topics.title: The Rise of Political Parties & Era of Jefferson
topics.cedContent: Supreme Court decisions (Marbury v. Madison, McCulloch v. Maryland) established the primacy of the judiciary and asserted implied powers. Following the Louisiana Purchase, the US government sought influence and control over North America through military, diplomatic, and economic means. The 'Revolution of 1800' was a peaceful transfer of power. The Embargo Act and War of 1812 reflected challenges of maintaining neutrality.

topics.title: Politics and Regional Interests
topics.cedContent: Congressional attempts at political compromise, such as the Missouri Compromise (1820), only temporarily stemmed growing tensions over the expansion of slavery. Regional economic interests diverged sharply: North favored manufacturing and tariffs; South depended on slavery and opposed tariffs; West wanted cheap land and transportation improvements. The nullification crisis revealed the tension between state and federal power.

topics.title: Market Revolution: Industrialization
topics.cedContent: Innovations including textile machinery, steam engines, interchangeable parts, the telegraph, and agricultural inventions contributed to the Market Revolution. Legislation and judicial systems supported development of roads, canals, and railroads. The Lowell System employed young women in New England textile mills. Increasing Southern cotton production and Northern manufacturing created growing economic interdependence and sectional tension.
illustrativeExamples: Eli Whitney's cotton gin (1793) and interchangeable parts

topics.title: Market Revolution: Society and Culture
topics.cedContent: The growth of manufacturing drove a significant increase in prosperity for some and the emergence of a middle class. Gender and family roles changed in response to the Market Revolution, particularly with the growth of definitions of domesticity separating home from workplace (Cult of Domesticity). Increasing numbers of factory workers no longer relied on semi-subsistence agriculture. Irish and German immigration transformed northern cities.

topics.title: Expanding Democracy
topics.cedContent: Democratic reforms in the 1820s–30s expanded voting rights for white men by eliminating property requirements. New political parties rose: Jackson's Democrats favored limited government and states' rights; the Whig Party opposed Jackson. The spoils system (patronage) replaced merit in government appointments. Increased voter participation and mass political rallies transformed American politics. Women, Black Americans, and Native peoples remained excluded.
illustrativeExamples: Removal of property requirements for white men

topics.title: Jackson and Federal Power
topics.cedContent: Frontier settlers championed expansion while American Indian resistance led to a sequence of wars and federal efforts to relocate Native peoples. Jackson's Bank War killed the Second Bank of the United States. The Indian Removal Act (1830) forced Cherokee, Creek, and other nations westward along the Trail of Tears. Jackson's actions revealed tensions between democratic rhetoric and the expansion of slavery and Native removal.
illustrativeExamples: Worcester v. Georgia (1832) — Jackson defied Supreme Court

topics.title: The Second Great Awakening
topics.cedContent: The Second Great Awakening emphasized moral improvement, social perfection, and personal salvation through revivalism. Camp meetings and Charles Finney's preaching spread in northern cities. The African Methodist Episcopal Church and other Black religious institutions developed. The revival linked to social reform movements including temperance, abolitionism, and women's rights. Utopian communities (Oneida, Brook Farm) reflected perfectionist ideals.
illustrativeExamples: Charles Finney's revivals in burned-over district

topics.title: An Age of Reform
topics.cedContent: Americans formed voluntary organizations aimed at changing individual behaviors and improving society through temperance, abolition, education, and prison reform. Abolitionist and antislavery movements gradually achieved emancipation in the North and contributed to the growth of the free Black community. Women's involvement in abolitionism contributed to the Seneca Falls Convention and early women's rights movement.

topics.title: The Society of the South in the Early Republic
topics.cedContent: Southern business leaders continued to rely on production and export of traditional agricultural staples (cotton, tobacco, rice, indigo). As overcultivation depleted arable land in the Southeast, slaveholders relocated plantations to more fertile lands in the Deep South and West, expanding slavery's reach. The defense of slavery evolved from 'necessary evil' to 'positive good.' Enslaved people resisted through both overt and covert means.
illustrativeExamples: Internal slave trade and 'Second Middle Passage'

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 5
topics.cedContent: Territorial expansion intensified debates over slavery's extension, ultimately fracturing the political system and leading to Civil War. Reconstruction attempted to redefine American democracy by extending citizenship and rights to formerly enslaved people, but ultimately fell short.

topics.title: Manifest Destiny
topics.cedContent: Advocates of annexing western lands argued that Manifest Destiny and the superiority of American institutions compelled the United States to expand. Westward migration was boosted by the passage of new legislation promoting western transportation and land sales. US interest in expanding trade led to economic, diplomatic, and cultural initiatives with Asia. Expansion displaced and destroyed Native peoples and led to conflict with Mexico.

topics.title: The Mexican-American War
topics.cedContent: US government interaction and conflict with Mexican Americans and American Indians increased in regions newly taken from Mexico. Questions about the status of slavery in newly acquired territories (Wilmot Proviso) reignited sectional tensions. Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo gave US a huge territory — California, New Mexico, Arizona. The war intensified the slavery debate more than any previous event.
illustrativeExamples: Wilmot Proviso (1846) — banned slavery in new territory
Californios lost land under US rule

topics.title: The Compromise of 1850
topics.cedContent: The Compromise of 1850 admitted California as a free state, applied popular sovereignty in Utah and New Mexico territories, strengthened the Fugitive Slave Act, and ended the slave trade in Washington DC. Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852) had enormous cultural impact on Northern opinion. The compromise only temporarily reduced sectional tensions without resolving the fundamental conflict over slavery's expansion.
illustrativeExamples: Fugitive Slave Act (1850) — required Northern compliance
Personal liberty laws in Northern states

topics.title: Failure of Compromise
topics.cedContent: The Second Party System ended when the issues of slavery and anti-immigrant nativism weakened loyalties to the two major parties. The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) repealed the Missouri Compromise's ban on slavery north of 36°30', leading to 'Bleeding Kansas' violence. The Republican Party formed on an anti-slavery-extension platform. Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857) ruled that Congress could not ban slavery in territories. John Brown's Harper's Ferry raid (1859) intensified Southern fears.

topics.title: Election of 1860 and Secession
topics.cedContent: Debates about secession centered on whether states had the right to leave the Union and whether slavery could be protected in the territories. Lincoln's election on an anti-slavery-extension platform triggered Deep South secession. Alexander Stephens's 'Cornerstone Speech' explicitly stated slavery was the 'cornerstone' of the Confederacy. Fort Sumter's attack (April 1861) began the war.
illustrativeExamples: Lincoln elected without a single Southern electoral vote

topics.title: Military Conflict in the Civil War
topics.cedContent: Although the Confederacy showed military initiative and daring early in the war, the Union ultimately succeeded due to its greater manpower and industrial resources. The Union employed a strategy of total war including the Anaconda Plan (naval blockade), Grant's brutal attrition strategy, and Sherman's March to the Sea. Antietam (Sept 1862) was the bloodiest single day and enabled the Emancipation Proclamation. Gettysburg (July 1863) was the turning point.
illustrativeExamples: King Cotton diplomacy failed — Britain stayed neutral

topics.title: Government Policies During the Civil War
topics.cedContent: Lincoln sought to reunify the country and used speeches such as the Gettysburg Address to portray the struggle against slavery as central to American identity. The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863) transformed the war's aims from Union preservation to freedom. African Americans served in the Union Army (180,000+) and played a crucial role. The 13th Amendment permanently abolished slavery. Federal power expanded dramatically through income tax, the Homestead Act, and Pacific Railroad Act.

topics.title: Reconstruction
topics.cedContent: The 13th Amendment abolished slavery, while the 14th and 15th amendments granted African Americans citizenship, equal protection, and voting rights. Radical Republicans sought to change the balance of power and restructure Southern society. Freedmen's Bureau provided education and assistance. African Americans participated in Southern politics — 16 Black congressmen, 2 Black senators. The women's rights movement was both emboldened and divided over the 14th and 15th amendments.

topics.title: Failure of Reconstruction
topics.cedContent: Segregation, violence, Supreme Court decisions, and local political tactics progressively stripped away African Americans' political rights and economic opportunities. The Compromise of 1877 withdrew federal troops from the South. Redeemer governments dismantled Reconstruction reforms. KKK terror suppressed Black political participation. The Supreme Court limited the 14th Amendment in the Civil Rights Cases (1883). Sharecropping and debt peonage replaced formal slavery.
illustrativeExamples: Compromise of 1877 — Hayes becomes president, troops withdrawn
Poll taxes, literacy tests, grandfather clauses

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 6
topics.cedContent: Rapid industrialization, technological innovation, and the rise of big business transformed the American economy after the Civil War. Massive immigration, urbanization, and labor conflict defined the Gilded Age, while farmers and workers sought political solutions to economic inequality.

topics.title: Westward Expansion: Social & Cultural Development
topics.cedContent: As migrant populations increased and the American bison population was decimated, competition for land and resources in the West intensified. The US government violated treaties with American Indians and responded to resistance with military force, eventually confining them to reservations. The Dawes Act (1887) broke up tribal land holdings through forced allotment. Many American Indians preserved their cultures and tribal identities despite assimilationist government policies.
illustrativeExamples: Dawes Act (1887) — broke up tribal land
Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show mythologized West

topics.title: The New South
topics.cedContent: The Supreme Court decision in Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upholding racial segregation (Jim Crow) helped mark the end of Reconstruction and the beginning of a long period of racial oppression. New South boosters promoted industrialization. Sharecropping and crop lien system kept freedmen in economic bondage. Convict leasing was a form of forced labor. Ida B. Wells documented lynching as a tool of racial terror.

topics.title: Technological Innovation
topics.cedContent: Technological innovations — Edison's electrical grid (lightbulb), Bell's telephone, Bessemer steel process, Carnegie's steel mills, Rockefeller's oil refining — drove the Second Industrial Revolution. Standardization of railroad tracks, interchangeable parts, and mass production transformed the economy. Cities were transformed by electricity, streetcars, and steel-frame skyscrapers.

topics.title: The Rise of Industrial Capitalism
topics.cedContent: Businesses made use of redesigned financial and management structures, advances in marketing, and a growing labor force. Many business leaders sought increased profits by consolidating corporations into large trusts and holding companies, which limited competition. Standard Oil (Rockefeller) used horizontal integration; Carnegie Steel used vertical integration. Social Darwinism and laissez-faire ideology justified inequality and resisted regulation.

topics.title: Labor in the Gilded Age
topics.cedContent: Labor and management battled over wages and working conditions, with workers organizing local and national unions and/or using strikes and boycotts. The industrial workforce expanded and child labor increased. Knights of Labor (inclusive) declined after Haymarket Riot (1886). AFL under Gompers focused on craft unions and 'bread and butter' issues. Government and military consistently sided with management against strikers.
illustrativeExamples: Haymarket Riot (1886) — destroyed Knights of Labor
Eugene Debs and American Railway Union
Child labor in mills and mines

topics.title: Immigration & Migration in the Gilded Age
topics.cedContent: As cities became areas of economic growth featuring new factories, they attracted immigrants from Asia and other parts of the world. 'New Immigration' from Southern and Eastern Europe (Italians, Poles, Russian Jews) transformed cities. Urban neighborhoods based on particular ethnicities provided new cultural opportunities. Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) was the first immigration restriction based on race.
illustrativeExamples: Jacob Riis: How the Other Half Lives (1890)

topics.title: Reform in the Gilded Age
topics.cedContent: Many women sought greater equality with men, joining voluntary organizations, going to college, and promoting social and political reform. The Farmers' Alliance united rural communities against railroad discrimination and banking practices. The Populist (People's) Party demanded silver coinage, railroad regulation, graduated income tax, and direct election of senators. Bryan's Cross of Gold speech (1896) made silver the central issue.
illustrativeExamples: William Jennings Bryan's Cross of Gold (1896)

topics.title: Controversies over the Role of Government
topics.cedContent: Foreign policymakers increasingly looked outside US borders to gain greater influence and control over markets and resources. Arguments for laissez-faire policies and competition dominated political discourse, with business leaders citing Social Darwinism. The Interstate Commerce Act (1887) was the first federal railroad regulation; the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) was mostly ineffective initially. Civil service reform (Pendleton Act) replaced the spoils system.
illustrativeExamples: Grover Cleveland vetoed farmer relief bill
Wabash v. Illinois (1886) — states can't regulate interstate RR

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 7
topics.cedContent: The United States emerged as a world power through imperialism, participation in two world wars, and economic dominance. Domestically, Progressive reform, the Great Depression, and the New Deal redefined the relationship between the federal government and American society.
illustrativeExamples: US entry into WWI and WWII

topics.title: Imperialism: Debates
topics.cedContent: Anti-imperialists cited principles of self-determination and invoked both racial theories and the US foreign policy tradition of non-intervention. Imperialists cited Social Darwinism, the 'White Man's Burden,' Mahan's sea power theory, and economic need for markets. The Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine asserted US right to intervene in Latin American nations to preserve stability and protect American interests.
illustrativeExamples: Mahan's The Influence of Sea Power (1890)

topics.title: The Spanish-American War
topics.cedContent: Yellow journalism (Hearst, Pulitzer) inflamed public opinion after the USS Maine explosion. The Teller Amendment renounced annexation of Cuba. The Treaty of Paris gave the US Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines. The Filipino-American War followed. The Platt Amendment gave the US the right to intervene in Cuba. The Insular Cases established that the Constitution could apply differently to territories.
illustrativeExamples: Rough Riders at San Juan Hill
Treaty of Paris (1898) — Philippines, Guam, Puerto Rico

topics.title: The Progressives
topics.cedContent: The Progressives were divided over many issues, including race — some supported segregation while others opposed it. On the national level, Progressives sought federal legislation to effectively regulate the economy and social welfare. Muckrakers exposed corporate abuses and government corruption. Political reforms included the 17th Amendment (direct election of senators), initiative, referendum, recall, and the direct primary. NAACP was founded (1909).

topics.title: World War I: Military and Diplomacy
topics.cedContent: Although the American Expeditionary Forces played a relatively limited role in combat, US entry helped tip the balance. Despite Wilson's deep involvement in postwar negotiations, the Senate refused to ratify the Treaty of Versailles or join the League of Nations. Germany's unrestricted submarine warfare, the Zimmermann Telegram, and economic ties to Britain pushed the US toward intervention.
illustrativeExamples: Treaty of Versailles — war guilt clause, reparations

topics.title: World War I: Home Front
topics.cedContent: Immigration from Europe peaked before World War I; during the war, nativist campaigns led to restriction quotas. The Great Migration of African Americans to Northern cities accelerated during WWI. Women entered the workforce in large numbers. The Espionage Act (1917) and Sedition Act (1918) suppressed dissent. The Red Scare (1919) targeted socialists, anarchists, and union organizers.
illustrativeExamples: Great Migration — 600,000 Black Americans north during WWI
Eugene Debs imprisoned for anti-war speech

topics.title: 1920s: Innovations in Communication & Technology
topics.cedContent: New forms of mass media (radio, cinema) contributed to the spread of national culture. New technologies and manufacturing techniques (Ford's assembly line, Fordism) increased productivity and mass consumption. The spread of automobile culture transformed American society — suburbanization, road construction, new industries. Prosperity was unevenly distributed: farmers and some workers did not share in the boom. Stock market speculation on credit laid the foundation for the 1929 crash.
illustrativeExamples: Ford's Model T and assembly line
First talking movie — The Jazz Singer (1927)
Black Swan Records — Black music industry

topics.title: 1920s: Cultural and Political Controversies
topics.cedContent: After WWI, nativist campaigns led to immigration restriction quotas. In the 1920s, cultural and political controversies emerged as Americans debated gender roles, modernism, science, and religion. The Harlem Renaissance saw an explosion of Black cultural production. 'Flappers' challenged traditional gender norms. Prohibition led to organized crime. The Scopes Trial pitted evolution against fundamentalism.
illustrativeExamples: Emergency Quota Act (1921) and National Origins Act (1924)
Ozawa v. US (1922) — Japanese not 'free white persons'

topics.title: The Great Depression
topics.cedContent: Episodes of credit and market instability, in particular the Great Depression, led to calls for greater government intervention in the economy. The stock market crash (1929), bank failures, Smoot-Hawley Tariff, and Hoover's inadequate response deepened the Depression. 25% unemployment, Dust Bowl, and Hoovervilles became defining images. Structural weaknesses: inequality, overproduction, easy credit, and international loan system.

topics.title: The New Deal
topics.cedContent: Although the New Deal did not end the Depression, it left a legacy of reforms and regulatory agencies and fostered a long-term association between the Democratic Party and the federal government. Banking reform (FDIC, Glass-Steagall), relief (CCC, FERA), recovery (NRA), and structural reform (Social Security, Wagner Act legalizing unions, SEC) dramatically expanded federal power. The court-packing controversy revealed limits of executive power.

topics.title: World War II: Mobilization
topics.cedContent: Mobilization provided opportunities for women and minorities to improve their socioeconomic positions for the war's duration. FDR issued Executive Order 9066, ordering over 100,000 Japanese-Americans interned (upheld in Korematsu v. US). Migration to the United States from Mexico increased through the Bracero Program. Women entered the workforce in large numbers ('Rosie the Riveter'). The Double V campaign demanded democracy abroad and at home.

topics.title: World War II: Military
topics.cedContent: The United States and its allies achieved military victory through Allied cooperation, technological and scientific advances, and overwhelming industrial capacity. D-Day (June 6, 1944) opened a second front and started the liberation of France. Island-hopping in the Pacific advanced US forces toward Japan. The atomic bombs at Hiroshima and Nagasaki ended the war with Japan. The Yalta Conference shaped the postwar world.
illustrativeExamples: Battle of Midway (1942) — Pacific turning point
Nuremberg Trials established war crimes precedent

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 8
topics.cedContent: The Cold War shaped American foreign and domestic policy for decades, while postwar prosperity and social movements transformed American society. Civil rights activism, the Great Society, and the counterculture challenged existing social hierarchies and redefined American identity.

topics.title: The Cold War from 1945 to 1980
topics.cedContent: As postwar tensions dissolved the wartime alliance, the US developed a strategy of containment to counter perceived Soviet expansionism. The US sought to contain communism through economic aid (Marshall Plan, Truman Doctrine), military alliances (NATO), and proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam). The Cold War fluctuated between periods of direct confrontation and mutual coexistence. Détente under Nixon-Kissinger and SALT I sought to reduce tensions.

topics.title: The Red Scare
topics.cedContent: Debates about exposing communists led to HUAC investigations, loyalty oaths, and the career of Senator Joseph McCarthy. The Alger Hiss case and Rosenbergs' execution heightened fears. McCarthy's televised Army-McCarthy Hearings (1954) led to his censure. The Hollywood Blacklist destroyed careers. Civil liberties vs. national security debate defined the era. Executive Order 9835 established loyalty oaths for federal employees.

topics.title: Economy after 1945
topics.cedContent: Federal spending on defense and infrastructure boosted postwar prosperity. The GI Bill provided veterans with housing loans, college education, and business loans, creating the suburban middle class. Baby boom and suburban growth (Levittown) transformed American life. Consumer culture flourished. Eisenhower warned of the military-industrial complex's growing influence in his farewell address. The seeds of inequality beneath prosperity — racial restrictions in FHA loans, redlining.
illustrativeExamples: GI Bill (1944) — college, housing for veterans
FHA and HOLC loans excluded Black Americans

topics.title: Early Steps in the Civil Rights Movement
topics.cedContent: The three branches of the federal government used measures including desegregation of the armed services (Executive Order 9981) and Brown v. Board of Education to advance the cause of civil rights. The NAACP used legal challenges (Thurgood Marshall). Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–56) launched MLK's career. Massive resistance in the South (Citizens' Councils, KKK) fought desegregation. Little Rock crisis (1957) tested federal enforcement power.

topics.title: The Vietnam War
topics.cedContent: Postwar decolonization and powerful nationalist movements in Asia led both sides in the Cold War to seek influence over emerging nations. The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (1964) gave LBJ broad war powers. Escalation created a quagmire; Tet Offensive (1968) undermined public support. My Lai massacre (1968) revealed the war's brutality. Nixon's Vietnamization and the Pentagon Papers (1971) revealed government deception. Americans debated appropriate presidential war-making power.

topics.title: The Great Society
topics.cedContent: Liberal ideas found expression in Lyndon Johnson's Great Society, which attempted to use federal legislation and programs to solve national problems. Civil Rights Act (1964) banned discrimination in public accommodations and employment. Voting Rights Act (1965) eliminated literacy tests and other barriers. Medicare and Medicaid extended healthcare. The Immigration Act (1965) ended national origins quotas and transformed US demographics.
illustrativeExamples: Civil Rights Act (1964) — Title II, Title VII
Immigration Act (1965) — ended national origins quotas

topics.title: The African American Civil Rights Movement (1960s)
topics.cedContent: Continuing resistance slowed desegregation, sparking social and political unrest across the nation. Sit-ins, Freedom Rides, and the Birmingham campaign (Bull Connor's violence) drew national attention. March on Washington (1963) and King's 'I Have a Dream.' Civil Rights Act (1964) and Voting Rights Act (1965) were major victories. Debates among civil rights activists: King's nonviolence vs. Malcolm X's Black nationalism vs. Black Power movement.

topics.title: The Civil Rights Movement Expands
topics.cedContent: Feminist and LGBTQ+ activists mobilized behind claims for legal, economic, and social equality. Feminists who participated in the counterculture rejected many social and political values of the 1950s. Latino movements (Chicano movement, UFW) and American Indian Movement (AIM) used the civil rights framework. The Stonewall Uprising (1969) launched the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement.
illustrativeExamples: César Chávez and UFW grape boycott
ERA passed Congress but not ratified

topics.title: Society in Transition
topics.cedContent: Public confidence and trust in government declined in the 1970s in the wake of Watergate, Vietnam, and economic stagflation. The 1970s saw growing clashes between conservatives and liberals over social and cultural issues, the power of the federal government, and economic policy. Stagflation (inflation + unemployment + energy crisis) shook confidence in Keynesian economics. The rise of New Right (Moral Majority, anti-ERA) set stage for Reagan.
illustrativeExamples: ERA defeated (3 states short of ratification)

topics.title: Contextualizing Period 9
topics.cedContent: The Reagan Revolution marked a conservative shift in American politics emphasizing limited government, free markets, and traditional values. The end of the Cold War left the US as sole superpower, while globalization, technological change, and demographic shifts created new economic and social challenges.

topics.title: Reagan and Conservatism
topics.cedContent: Conservatives argued that liberal programs were counterproductive in fighting poverty and stimulating economic growth. Reaganomics: supply-side tax cuts, deregulation, reduced domestic spending, and increased defense spending. Policy debates continued over free-trade agreements, the scope of government, and social issues. Intense cultural debates continued over immigration, gender roles, and family values. The New Right coalition united fiscal conservatives, social conservatives, and defense hawks.
illustrativeExamples: Economic Recovery Tax Act (1981) — largest tax cut

topics.title: The End of the Cold War
topics.cedContent: Increased US military spending, Reagan's diplomatic initiatives, and political changes and economic problems in Eastern Europe contributed to the end of the Cold War. Gorbachev's glasnost and perestroika reforms weakened Soviet control. Fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) and Soviet collapse (1991) left the US as sole superpower. The end of the Cold War led to new diplomatic relationships but also new US military interventions.

topics.title: A Changing Economy
topics.cedContent: Technological innovations in computing, digital mobile technology, and the internet transformed daily life, increased access to information, and affected the economy. Employment increased in service sectors and decreased in manufacturing (deindustrialization), and union membership declined. Real wages stagnated for the working and middle class amid growing economic inequality. Globalization through NAFTA (1994) and WTO accelerated these trends.

topics.title: Migration and Immigration in the 1990s and 2000s
topics.cedContent: International migration from Latin America and Asia increased dramatically. New immigrants affected US culture in many ways, sparking intense debates over assimilation, bilingual education, and immigration policy. The Immigration Reform and Control Act (1986) granted amnesty to some undocumented immigrants. Nativist resentment arose citing economic competition. Demographic transformation toward multiracial America accelerated.
illustrativeExamples: Southwest population growth from Latin American immigration

topics.title: Challenges of the 21st Century
topics.cedContent: The War on Terror sought to improve security within the United States but raised questions about the protection of civil liberties. Conflicts in the Middle East and concerns about climate change led to debates about US energy policy. Despite challenges, the United States continued as the world's leading superpower. The 2008 financial crisis and the Obama administration's response shaped debates about government's economic role.
